coredata.dc:title: Facile green synthesis and characterization of fluorescent nanoprobe based on Ca, K, N-doped carbon quantum dots derived from Aegle marmelos for bioimaging of human neuroblastoma cancer cells and living organisms
coredata.dc:creator: Kansay, Vishal
Sharma, Varun Dutt
Chandan, G.
Sharma, Indu
Bhatia, Anita
Chakrabarti, S.
Bera, M.K.
coredata.dc:description: This study demonstrated an environmentally responsible, inexpensive and simple one-step hydrothermal method to synthesize biocompatible fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) from Aegle marmelos fruit extract. The near-spherical CQDs were discovered to have a naturally distributed size of 3–6 nm, were unintentionally doped with heteroatoms (Ca, K, and N) without the need for doping element precursors due to mineral contents in extract, had a quantum yield of about 4.06%, strong photostability, and excellent water solubility. Several morphological, compositional, physicochemical, and optical properties were studied. Other variables influencing CQD photoluminescence, such as hydrothermal process parameters and pH sensitivity, were also examined. Zeta potential analysis showed a negative surface charge with a zeta potential value of −5.29 mV and good dispersibility in aqueous solution. Furthermore, as an alternative to organic or synthetic probes, there is a huge need for the development of simple, affordable, and non-destructive fluorescent biomarkers. As-synthesized Ca, K, N-doped CQDs have demonstrated good fluorescent staining abilities and may be employed as a helpful probe for bio-imaging of cancer cells and other organisms. Cell survival and cytotoxicity were found to be satisfactory, with 90% of SH-SY5Y and HCT-116 cells surviving, compared to 63–77% for the other organisms, even at higher dosages (200 µg/mL) for up to 4 days.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: On the other hand, Aegle marmelos-derived carbon quantum dots were found to include a variety of functional groups and interactions, as shown in Fig. 4(d). Stretching frequencies of 3298.7, 2185.16, 1635.13, 1506.88, and 1060.16 cm−1, respectively, strongly supported the existence of -OH, –CN, –C=C=C-, N–H, N–O, and C–N. The hydroxyl and carboxylic groups can be found on the surfaces of CQDs, according to all of the studies above [34–36]. As-synthesized CQDs are highly water soluble due to the presence of these hydrophilic functional groups.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study demonstrated an environmentally responsible, inexpensive and simple one-step hydrothermal method to synthesize biocompatible fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) from Aegle marmelos fruit extract. The near-spherical CQDs were discovered to have a naturally distributed size of 3–6 nm, were unintentionally doped with heteroatoms (Ca, K, and N) without the need for doping element precursors due to mineral contents in extract, had a quantum yield of about 4.06%, strong photostability, and excellent water solubility. Several morphological, compositional, physicochemical, and optical properties were studied. Other variables influencing CQD photoluminescence, such as hydrothermal process parameters and pH sensitivity, were also examined. Zeta potential analysis showed a negative surface charge with a zeta potential value of −5.29 mV and good dispersibility in aqueous solution. Furthermore, as an alternative to organic or synthetic probes, there is a huge need for the development of simple, affordable, and non-destructive fluorescent biomarkers. As-synthesized Ca, K, N-doped CQDs have demonstrated good fluorescent staining abilities and may be employed as a helpful probe for bio-imaging of cancer cells and other organisms. Cell survival and cytotoxicity were found to be satisfactory, with 90% of SH-SY5Y and HCT-116 cells surviving, compared to 63–77% for the other organisms, even at higher dosages (200 µg/mL) for up to 4 days.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent probes have become indispensable chemical tools in chemical biology and medicine. The ability to identify intracellular species and monitor physiological processes has not only increased the knowledge of biology, but also provided new approaches to disease diagnosis. Bioimaging is one of the most viable techniques for observing live cells in numerous disciplines of biomedicine and clinical research utilizing fluorescence microscopy [1,2]. This enables real-time monitoring of biological activities. To visualize the cells/species in the sample, bioimaging investigations frequently need the use of a fluorescent probe or a particular biomarker [3]. Imaging is essential nowadays in modern biomedicine for identifying cell structure and function, detecting biomarkers in diagnosis, tracking the course of current sickness, treating various malignancies, and assessing therapy success. Besides, fluorescent probes have been used effectively in immunofluorescence labelling, live-cell imaging, drug delivery, fluorescence-guided surgery etc. [4]. However, significant obstacles remain due to the complexity of biological processes and the restricted tissue penetration depth of light. An ideal fluorescent probe must have high cell/tissue specificity, resilient selectivity for the target biomarker in the presence of competing molecules, photostability, excellent biocompatibility, and long-wavelength excitation (essential for in vivo imaging).

Many organic fluorophores/dyes used in bioimaging include Cy3, fluorescein, 5-carboxyfluorescein, Cy5, carboxytetramethylrhodamine, fluorescein isothiocyanate, rhodamine B, etc. are commercially available with very high quantum yields of up to 80% [5]. However, traditional organic fluorophores still have several disadvantages that prevent their use for high-sensitivity detection and real-time monitoring, especially low fluorescence intensity, week-long photostability and insufficient stability [6].Fluorescent nanoparticles alleviate some of the limitations of organic fluorescent dyes, such as poor hydrophilicity, rapid photobleaching, and low quantum yield.

In this regard, fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQD) have created a new class of carbon-related nanomaterials since the discovery of single-walled carbon nanotubes in an electrophoretic purification process in 2004 due to their extraordinary physicochemical properties and many potential applications [7,8]. CQDs have been successfully used in biological imaging, drug delivery, and many other therapeutic and biomedical applications because they can be easily functionalized with biomolecules, are less toxic, and have inert chemical properties [9,10]. Reproducibility remains a challenge, although semiconductor quantum dots are often produced using inexpensive precursors [11,12]. Therefore, it is necessary to research and develop a simple, cost-effective and environmentally friendly approach based on affordable starting materials.

In the search for an efficient, inexpensive, and environmentally acceptable CQD synthesis method, natural biomaterials are usually chosen over alternative organic, inorganic, or synthetic materials. Several recent studies on the production of CQDs using natural precursors have been reported [13–15]. Traditional physical and chemical approaches, on the other hand, are significantly less acceptable than green synthesis methods. Bottom-up hydrothermal technology using natural raw materials has attracted much attention today due to its environmental friendliness, ease of use, low cost, and mass production. The presence of minerals in natural biomaterials also enables doping of heteroatoms without the need for dopant precursors.

One of the primary goals of this research is to establish a simple and successful experimental approach for producing low-cost CQDs utilizing a one-step hydrothermal synthesis of Aegle marmelos fruit extract, also known as bael. Aegle marmelos is an economically useful tree. People like the juicy fruit pulp of ripe bael fruits, which is ideal for making jam, syrup, and pudding. Because of its medicinal characteristics, bael is also used in ayurvedic herbal medicine treatments [16]. Using fluorescent biomarkers, fluorescent labelling of various living species such as bacteria, fungi, plant cells and so on is possible. The use of biocompatible, non-toxic, environmentally friendly and economically affordable fluorescent materials is essential for a sustainable future.

In this study, we demonstrated the important material and optical properties of CQDs synthesized from Aegle marmelos fruit extract and fluorescence staining technique to use these CQDs as fluorescent markers in bioimaging of cancer cells and other living organisms.

Ripe bael fruit (scientific name– Aegle marmelos, botanical family– Rutaceae) as a precursor for CQDs was taken from the nearby locality of the University campus (GPS locations: 30°15′4″N, 77°2′39″E). Fresh lemon juice was extracted for functionalizing the natural carbon quantum dots. 3-(4, 5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5diphenyltetra-zolium bromide (MTT) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Aqua regia solution (HCL: HNO3 in 3:1) was used to clean the glassware. All aqueous solutions were prepared using double distilled deionized water which was obtained using a Milli-Q system (18.2 ΩM.cm−1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of carbon quantum dots from Aegle marmelos
ce:section.ce:para: In a one-step hydrothermal process, CQDs were synthesized using Aegle marmelos as a precursor. The Aegle marmelos fruits were first harvested and rinsed with distilled water before being peeled and crushed into juice in a domestic pastel mortar. In a glass vial, the extracted juice was mixed with freshly prepared lemon juice in a 7:3 ratio. Lemon juice was used for surface functionalization purposes.

The mixture was then placed in an ultrasonic bath for 20 min for the shaking of the well mix. The solution was then placed in a hydrothermal autoclave with a Teflon liner that carried 50 mL. This operation took 45 min–720 min at temperatures ranging from 110 to 200 °C. After cooling to room temperature, the as-prepared solution was centrifuged for 10 min between 8000 and 9000 rpm. After centrifugation, the pollutants settled and the supernatant solution was collected. In order to remove any leftover impurities not eliminated by centrifugation, the supernatant solution was filtered using a 0.22 micro-metre syringe filter made of polytetrafluoroethylene membrane. After filtering, the solution was freeze-dried in a lyophilizer at −40 °C and kept for future use. The pH of the as-prepared solution was around 3.47. Fig. 1 depicts the entire method graphically.

High-resolution transmission electron microscopy was used to characterise the morphology of the CQDs. JEOL JEM 2100 plus with 200 kV accelerating voltage was used to capture the high-resolution TEM images. Shimadzu IRSpirit FTIR was used for the Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy investigation, with a resolution of 16 cm −1 and scanning wavelength spans from 400 to 4000 cm−1. X-ray diffraction (D8 DISCOVER Bruker) with Ni-filtered CuKα1 radiation was used to examine the crystal structure and crystallinity of the CQDs.

The scanning was performed from 2θ = 10 to 80° with a step of 0.02° at 30 kV. Using a SpectraMax iD3 spectrophotometer, the UV–visible spectroscopy and fluorescence spectroscopic analyses of CQDs were recorded. The wavelength range of 230–750 nm was used for absorption spectroscopy, and the emission wavelength range of 290–750 nm was used for fluorescence spectroscopy. Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy was used to examine the composition and elemental analyses of the CQDs. JEOL JEM-2100Fs were used to collect the EDX measurements. The Anton Paar Litesizer 500 was used to measure the surface charges of CQDs. The CPR-30 Plus centrifuge was used to ultra-centrifuge the sample. The 13C and 1H nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopic experiments were performed on a 500 MHz Bruker Advance Neo NMR spectrometer. The binding energies and elemental composition were determined using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy on a Physical Electronics PHI 5000 Versa Probe III with a range of 0–1200 eV.

The relative quantum yield values (ɸ) for quinine sulphate dye, used as a reference standard material, for Aegle marmelos fruit derived CQDs were evaluated. The measurement details can be found from our previous publication [17]. The quantum yield obtained was around 4.06%.

The National Centre for Cell Studies in Pune, India provided the human neuroblastoma SH-SY5Y and the human colon cancer HCT-116 cell line. Cell lines were grown until confluent on Dulbecco's modified eagle medium with 10% fetal bovine serum, streptomycin, and penicillin (1%) in a stringent CO2 atmosphere (5%) at 37 °C. Used medium were discarded, and dead cells and debris were removed with a phosphate buffered saline wash. Cells were trypsinized with 0.25% trypsin. After another 5 min of centrifugation at 2500 rpm, the pellet was cultured. Thereafter in order to prepare new aliquots for sub-culturing, they were moved to fresh culture Petri dishes.

To investigate the effects of CQDs, SH-SY5Y cells (approximately 1x104) were seeded into a 12 well plate and incubated for 24 h. After 24 h, the cells were washed with 1X PBS and stained for 30 min with Ca,K,N-doped CQDs at a concentration of 100 μg/mL. The pH was adjusted to a range of 7.4 before CQDs were used to stain the cells. The cells were finally rinsed twice. Following that, the cells were incubated for 30 min. The photos were taken with a fluorescence microscope (Carl Zeiss, Axio Vert A1, Germany).

In a study on in vitro biocompatibility, the cell lines SH-SY5Y and HCT-116 were used. The cell viability tests were repeated for three times. Cells were seeded into a 96-well plate with ∼1x104 cells in each well before the test. After that, the cells were exposed to Ca,K,N-doped CQDs diluted in medium at varied doses the following day in order to evaluate their biocompatibility and cytotoxicity. CQDs were not used on the cells in the control group. The MTT assay was used to measure cell viability after the cells had been incubated for 24 h. The cell cytotoxicity (CC) was calculated using the formula below:
CC % = [(Acontrol - Asample)/ Acontrol ] x 100, where Acontrol and Asample respectively are the absorbance of the untreated and treated cultures.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Staining procedure of bioimaging of living organisms
ce:section.ce:para: For this investigation, we used bioimaging with mushroom (Agaricus bisporus), bacteria (Staphylococcus aureus), fungus (Mucor circinelloides) and pine plant leaves (Pinus roxburghii) for bioimaging. Sharp blades were utilized to slice the mushrooms and pine leaves into significant amounts that could float into DI water for slide processing. With a soft brush, they are carefully collected and placed in a watch glass filled with de-ionized water. Fine portions are extracted with a brush and placed in a drop of water on a slide before being examined under an optical microscope. After that, the fluorescent CQD are only utilized to stain plant surface and mushrooms. Due of this, the area was initially placed on a glass slide. The section slices were fully stained with a Ca, K, N-doped CQD-based alternative fluorescent stain from Aegle marmelos (∼25 μg/mL), and then they were covered with a coverslip and examined under a fluorescence microscope. The same process was used to deposit fluorescent Ca, K, N-doped CQDs on a glass slide, followed by a tuft of fungal mycelium spores and a coverslip. The slide was then viewed under a fluorescence microscope. Prior to air drying, the bacterial smear was stained with fluorescent Ca, K, N-doped CQDs. A sliding rack was used to keep the smears in place. The smear was then cautiously treated with the glowing stain.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biocompatibility and cytotoxicity study in living organisms
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence of CQDs were utilized to investigate biocompatibility with a various materials such as mushroom, bacteria, fungus, and pine plant. The in-vitro experiments were carried over a four-day period at various (∼25 μg/mL, ∼50 μg/mL, ∼100 μg/mL, and ∼200 μg/mL) doses. Before starting the experiment pine and mushroom epidermal layers were placed on a slide. Similarly, we examine the survivability of fungus and bacteria at above-mentioned dosage.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological, structural and physicochemical studies of Ca,K,N-doped CQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
HRTEM analysis was used to determine the size and morphological distribution of as-synthesized CQDs as can be seen from Fig. 2
(a)-(b). It implies that the particles are spherical, monodisperse, and independent of any aggregation. The particle size histogram shown in Fig. 2(c) reveals that CQDs have a narrow size distribution centred approximately 4.39 ± 1.64 nm, with an average size of 4.39 nm. The distributions are clearly bimodal. This is because quantum dots have a roughly spherical form and the number of graphite layers determines their thickness. However, the number of graphite layer and particle diameter distribution are overlaid to produce the TEM size distributions [18,19]. As a result of the distribution being impacted by the orientation of the CQDs on the TEM grid, the variability is increased. Besides, the SAED pattern provides additional evidence that the synthesized CQDs are poor crystalline in nature as can be seen from Fig. 2 (d).

The CQDs were further analysed using EDX spectroscopy to determine their chemical makeup. The presence of high carbon (at. percent 76.35) concentration as well as tracer amounts of oxygen (at. percent 14.45), nitrogen (at. percent 7.9), potassium (at. percent 1.01), and calcium (at. percent 0.29) concentrations in the EDX spectra shown in Fig. 2(e), suggests that the as-synthesized CQDs are unintentionally doped with N, K, and Ca. The presence of distinct functionalization groups is indicated by the higher oxygen concentrations seen on the surface of CQDs (such as hydroxyl, carbonyl, or carboxyl groups). Numerous articles in the literature claim that the chemical structure of doped-CQDs, including surface functionalization, the amount of doping elements, and the placement of dopant atoms, has an impact on the optical properties of these materials (whether they are within the carbon core or on the surface) [20]. Although unintentional, however, it will be useful to investigate the fluorescence properties of heteroatom doped CQDs.

Furthermore, XPS was utilized to investigate the chemical composition of CQDs. The XPS survey spectrum is displayed in Fig. 3(a). The survey spectra revealed prominent peaks at 283.7, 530.6, 398.7, 291.9, and 347.1 eV, corresponding to C 1s, O 1s, N 1s, K 2p, and Ca 2p, indicating the presence of carbon, oxygen, nitrogen, potassium, and calcium. The atomic percentages of C, O, N, K, and Ca are around 65.06, 25.84, 7.60, 0.99, and 0.51%, respectively, according to estimates comparable with the EDX investigation.

In addition, high resolution C 1s and K 2p spectra were deconvoluted into five peaks that emerged at 283.7, 285.1, 287.1, 291.7, and 295.1 eV, respectively, and originated from sp2C-sp2C, N-sp2C, C–OH,
K
2 p
3 / 2
and
K
2 p
1 / 2
, respectively (see Fig. 3(b)). The O 1s spectrum in Fig. 3(c) is also found to include two components with centres at 530.6 and 531.6 eV, indicating the presence of the C=O and C–OH groups [21–23]. The strong N 1s spectra shown in Fig. 3(d) may be deconvoluted into three peaks that, respectively, correspond to pyrrolic/pyridinic, quaternary, and O=C–N groups and show that nitrogen atoms were effectively doped in CQDs. According to earlier studies, graphitic N improves the electronic cloud density on CQD surfaces, which boosts the luminescence effectiveness. In accordance with this, Ca 2p peaks (Fig. 3(e)) at 347.1 and 349.8 eV indicate effective Ca doping in CQDs [24,25].

As a result, the XPS analysis shows that the inexpensive and environmentally friendly synthesis of Ca,K,N-doped CQDs from Aegle marmelos was effective without the need for additional sources of doping elements. Overall, the generated CQDs had a variety of O- and N-related functional groups, which may have contributed to their fluorescence emission in addition to being the cause of their high water solubility.

XRD analysis was used to confirm the crystallinity of the as-synthesized Ca,K,N-doped CQDs. The observed broad diffraction peak in Fig. 4(a) with a centre angle of 23.1° indicates the (002) plane, this was linked to CQDs' hexagonal graphite structure's interlayer packing of uneven carbon atoms, resulting in a turbostratic structure. A minor rise at 42.9° suggests that graphitization has occurred along the (100) plane (JCPDS Card No. 26–1076) [26]. The observed XRD is quite similar to those have been reported in the literature [27–31]. Thus, XRD results show that efficient N,K,Ca doping improves the crystalline form of otherwise amorphous CQDs. The sp3-and sp2-composition of the core was investigated using nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectroscopy. Carbon quantum dots have been demonstrated to interact with sp3 and sp2 carbon atoms. The hybridized carbon atoms with sp3 and sp2 were located using 13C NMR. The sp3-and sp2-hybridized carbon atoms are present in a mixture, as seen by the 13C NMR spectra in Fig. 4(b), with sp3-hybridized carbon atoms predominating.

There were four distinct regions found, namely 20–80 ppm (for sp3 carbons as well as carbons attached with hydroxyl groups), 80–100 ppm (for carbons attached with ether linkages), 100–120 ppm (for C=C aromatic or sp2 hybridized carbon atoms), and finally 175–190 ppm (for C=O carbons) [32]. The following chemical shift (δ) regions were discovered in the 1H NMR spectrum as shown in Fig. 4(c): 1–3 ppm (dominated by sp3 C–H protons), 3–6 ppm (associated with protons of hydroxyl, ether, and carbonyl groups), 6–8 ppm (for aromatic or sp2 protons), and 8–10 ppm (related to aldehydic protons) [33].

On the other hand, Aegle marmelos-derived carbon quantum dots were found to include a variety of functional groups and interactions, as shown in Fig. 4(d). Stretching frequencies of 3298.7, 2185.16, 1635.13, 1506.88, and 1060.16 cm−1, respectively, strongly supported the existence of -OH, –CN, –C=C=C-, N–H, N–O, and C–N. The hydroxyl and carboxylic groups can be found on the surfaces of CQDs, according to all of the studies above [34–36]. As-synthesized CQDs are highly water soluble due to the presence of these hydrophilic functional groups.

Determination of surface charge and stability in suspension are another key aspects of CQDs which were explored with a zetasizer. The characteristics of CQDs, such as their double layer structure and ability to include a variety of hydrophilic or hydrophobic functional groups, are also revealed by this approach. The stability of CQDs particles over short or extended time periods determines by zeta potential. A zeta potential of −5.29 mV was obtained, as shown in Fig. 4(e), indicating that CQDs are fairly stable in aqueous solution.

Hydrothermal process conditions have a large influence on the formation of quantum dots of diverse sizes and distributions, and hence on their optical characteristics. We looked deeper into the effects of process factors such as temperature and time on as-synthesized Ca,K,N-doped CQDs. The impact of process condition was evident when the threshold absorption peaks were increasingly pushed towards longer wavelengths, from 416.7 nm for 110 °C to 428.7 nm for 200 °C process temperature, as illustrated in Fig. 5 (a), while the process time for all was maintained at 3 h. The red-shift in peak absorption is thought to be produced by the substantially bigger particle size of CQDs created as a result of favourable nucleation conditions at higher processing temperatures. This is owing to the n- π* transitions in the graphitic core and the usual π - π* transition in the conjugated C=C band [37,38].

Meanwhile, the standard Tauc plot was used to estimate the optical band gap. The optical band gap was measured between 3.195 and 3.359 eV, with hydrothermal process temperatures variations from 110 to 200 °C for a fixed duration of 3 h. The details of the calculation can be found in the supplementary material (Fig. S1). After optimizing the process temperature, the influence of process time was also investigated. Nonetheless, a similar trend was observed for process time variation from 45 min to 720 min while keeping the process temperature constant at 200 °C, as illustrated in Fig. 5(b). It is anticipated that sufficient agglomeration/nucleation will occur for a longer period of time, causing particle size to increase and, as a result, absorption peaks to move to higher wavelengths. Moreover, the supplemental data as shown in Fig. S2 also demonstrates how the conventional Tauc plot was used to evaluate the change in optical energy band gap caused by time-dependent process variations, which ranged from 2.899 to 2.954 eV for 45 and 720 min process durations. Nonetheless, the hydrothermal process parameters were optimized to be 110 °C and 3 h based on consistency and repeatability, as well as UV–Vis and fluorescence characteristics.

Carbon quantum dots, on the other hand, are usually distinguished by their size-dependent photoluminescence activity and emission wavelength. Hence, fluorescence emission regarding CQDs process parameters have also been examined. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra of Ca,K,N-doped CQDs dispersed in water are illustrated in Fig. 6 (a), and it was observed that the PL emission spectra are hydrothermal process dependent. At lower process temperatures, the PL emission spectra showed a wider asymmetric peak range from 338.5 to 376.7 nm following excitation with a wavelength of 250 nm, but it became sharper and more intense as temperature climbed from 110 to 200 °C [39,40].

This is further demonstrated by the normalized PL characteristics shown in Fig. 6(a). The normalized deconvoluted fluorescence spectra, shown in Supplemental Fig. S3 and Table S1, revealed three distinct broad peaks at process temperatures ranging from 110 to 200 °C. It can be observed that the leading peak shifted towards higher emission wavelength as the hydrothermal process temperature rises from 110 to 200 °C. The obtained PL spectra may be deconvoluted into four peaks, with the dominant peak finding to move towards higher wavelengths from its centre at 361 nm–374 nm. However, the second and third most significant peak contributions were observed to move somewhat lower in wavelength from its centre at 438 nm–436 nm and from 545 nm to 335 nm, respectively. Fig. S3 provides details of the deconvoluted PL spectra. Meanwhile, the asymmetric widening of the PL spectra was visible in the normalized PL spectra, as shown in Fig. 6(a). As a consequence, the temperature-dependent PL spectra clearly indicates multi-colour fluorescence emission, which appears to be produced by a rise in the degree of carbonization and shape change of CQDs as process temperature increases.

The influence of process duration on PL emission was also investigated for the optimized hydrothermal process temperature at 110 °C. As can be clearly observed the normalized PL spectra in Fig. 6(c), the PL emission spectra widened asymmetrically after excitation with a 280 nm wavelength when the process time was short, but sharpened and intensified when the process time was extended to 12 h. Yet again, the normalized deconvoluted fluorescence spectra exhibited three different wide peaks for varied process durations, as shown in Supplemental Fig. S4 (a)–(e) and Table S2. The following observations were noticed as the hydrothermal process duration increased from 45 to 720 min, (i) contributed peak widened and shifted to a higher wavelength from 358 nm to 460 nm, (ii) the second intermediate contributed peak shifted to a shorter wavelength from 509 nm to 361 nm, but (iii) the third intermediate pea was shifted to a higher wavelength from 415 nm to 563 nm. However, (iv) the lowest contributed peak was relocated to 330 nm from 326 nm.

In general, experiments of PL emission generally find greenish fluorescence emission. Ca,K,N-doped CQDs dispersed in aqueous solution fluoresces intensely and vividly green when subjected to UV light (365 nm, 60 W), as seen in Fig. 6(d). Additionally, aqueous solution of Ca,K,N-doped CQDs revealed a stable colloidal solution without any precipitation.

Another unique feature of CQDs is pH sensitive fluorescence. The fluorescence behaviour was studied at various pH levels, and the CQDs performed well in terms of pH sensitivity or stability. The normalized PL spectra of CQDs is measured from acidic to basic medium (pH: 2–12) as demonstrated in Fig. 7 . There corresponding deconvolution spectrum exhibited three different wide peaks for varied process durations, as shown in Supplemental Fig. S5 (a)–(k) and Table S3.

In fact, a minor blue shift was seen in the pH-responsive PL spectra of the majority of contributing PL emissions, with the main peak at 463 nm @pH:2 shifting into 443 nm @pH:12, most likely due to reversible protonation and deprotonation of functional groups such as carboxylic and amide groups.

A considerable deal of scientific attention has always been focused on luminescent nanoparticles because they have wide range of applications in the field of biosensing, bioimaging, display technologies etc. Particularly those with emission wavelength controllability might be quite beneficial. The emission wavelengths were expanded from 336 to 614 nm as the excitation wavelengths increased from 250 to 550 nm in a step of 15 nm, as shown in Fig. 8 .

The observed excitation-wavelength dependent PL is believed to be caused by the quantum confinement effect, often known as the size effect according to the widely recognized hypothesis [41–43]. CQD energy gaps are specifically determined by particle shapes and sizes, a smaller size leads to a larger energy gap and deviation of energy gaps produced by size distribution induces enlarged emission wavelengths in the range of 380–589 nm. In addition, the presence of various surface functional groups (carboxylic, –COOH, hydroxyl, –OH, etc.) at the shell, dangling bonds at the surface, oxygen associated localized states as well as various heteroatoms (here unintentionally doped N, K, and Ca-atoms) are likely to play the function of numerous fluorescence centres in excitation dependent PL emission.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of Aegle marmelos derived Ca,K,N-doped CQDs as a biomarker
Cell imaging of human neuroblastoma SH-SY5Y cells and cytotoxicity assay
ce:section.ce:para:
The triple-subcloned cell line SH-SY5Y was derived from the neuroblastoma cell line SK-N-SH. Since the cells may be changed into different kinds of functioning neurons by the addition of particular substances, it acts as a model for neurodegenerative diseases. In vitro cellular imaging of SH-SY5Y cells was explored using Ca,K,N-doped CQDs as fluorescent probe. For these purposes, 100 μg/mL Ca,K,N-doped CQDs were incubated with neuroblastoma cells for the duration specified in the experimental section before they were prepared for fluorescence microscopy detection. The Ca,K,N-doped CQDs exhibited improved cellular uptake in SH-SY5Y cells within the allotted time. In the bright field, the profile of SH-SY5Y cells is just minimally visible, as illustrated in Fig. 9 (a)-(f). Nonetheless, SH-SY5Y cells exhibited multi-colour fluorescence, with bright green fluorescence evenly distributed throughout the cells, as seen in Fig. 9 (g)–(l) under green filter having λ
em = 525 nm, and reddish under red filter having λ
em = 615 nm (Fig. 9 (m)–(r)). It is notable that a relatively low dose (100 μg/mL) is adequate to probe SH-SY5Y with bright greenish fluorescence.

Besides photoluminescence, CQDs must have high biocompatibility and low toxicity if they are to be developed as a potential bio-labelling reagent. Materials used in biology must be exceedingly biocompatible at tolerable concentrations [44,45]. For this purposes, the in vitro cytotoxic response of numerous cell lines were evaluated using the high-throughput cell-based MTT (3-(4,5-dimethylthiazolyl-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyl tetrazolium bromide) assay. MTT assay is a common colorimetric assay (an experiment that gauges changes in colour) used in laboratories to assess cellular proliferation or growth. In order to examine cytotoxicity, two-different cell lines, SH-SY5Y and HCT-116 (human colon cancer cells) were cultured in an incubator at 37 °C and 5% CO2 for 24 h with Ca,K,N-doped CQDs at varied concentrations ranging from 0 to 200 μg/mL.

MTT assay studies, as shown in Fig. 10 , revealed that CQDs were basically non-toxic, with more than 90% of cells surviving for HCT-116 cells while over 80% of cells surviving for SH-SY5Y tumors. These results clearly demonstrate that the newly synthesized Ca,K,N-doped CQDs from Aegle marmelos fruit extracts with excited greenish fluorescence have low cytotoxicity and excellent biocompatibility, which bodes well for their prospective usage in biological imaging.

Ca,K,N-doped CQDs were utilized as fluorescent markers in the bioimaging of diverse mushrooms, bacteria, fungus, and plant leaves, as illustrated in Fig. 11 . The white button mushroom was used in this investigation (Agaricus bisporus). Bacteria (Staphylococcus aureus), fungus (Mucor circinelloides), and pine leaves (Pinus roxburghii) were also employed in the bioimaging tests. A mushroom mainly comprises of a stem, a cap, and gills. Mushroom gills are commonly examined under a microscope to identify mushroom taxonomy [46,47]. The most important microscopic feature for recognizing mushrooms is their spores. Mushroom spores are often identified based on their colour, shape, size, attachment, and responsiveness to chemical analysis. The boundaries of the gills are plainly visible under the fluorescent microscope after successfully staining with CQDs. The hymenial layer is formed by the gill edges and generates the spore-bearing structures known as basidia as can be seen from Fig. 11(a)–(d). There are many spores i.e. basidiospores visible on the surface of this layer or free between the gills. Staphylococcus aureus, on the other hand, is a prominent human pathogen that causes a wide range of illnesses, from minor skin infections to life-threatening disorders including endocarditis, osteomyelitis etc. [48–51].

The cell wall of Staphylococcus aureus is complex, with a thick peptidoglycan layer and a polysaccharide capsule. Furthermore, S. aureus contains a complex arsenal of structural and secreted virulence components. S. aureus is a gram-positive bacterium with a spherical form (0.5–2 μm in diameter). They are most typically seen in the mouth and respiratory system mucous membranes, where they have been linked to a variety of illnesses and infections, including sepsis, pneumonia, and pharyngitis [52,53]. Since S. aureus is the most prevalent infection, it will be advantageous to have a simple fluorescence detection method. Therefore, Staphylococcus aureus was selected for bioimaging study. However, due to low magnification, S. aureus shows a flashing of fluorescent green colour emission after staining with Ca,K,N-doped CQDs, as shown in Fig. 11(e)–(h).

Furthermore, Mucor circinelloides is a naturally occurring fungus that has the potential to cause mucormycosis in immunocompromised patients. It may be found all around the world, particularly in soil, manure, and root crops. Mucor circinelloides colonies expand rapidly, changing from cottony to fluffy, white to yellow, and finally dark-grey as sporangia mature. Sporangia are typically globose or sub-globose with a slightly flattened base, and they range in length from 60 to 300 μm [54]. The sporangia start off being white before turning black with many spores, however they never hang over. Thousands of spherical sporangiospores can be seen inside the sporangia. After fluorescent staining with Ca, K, N-doped CQDs, cottony colonies are clearly visible on the slide as a fluorescent colour region with elongated sporangiophores formed of bigger spherical sporangia, as shown in Fig. 11(i)–(l). When images were captured with a green filter or a ref filter, the cottony colonies demonstrated a strong green fluorescence or a reddish fluorescence.

Besides, Fig. 11(m)–(p) shows the anatomy of a pine leaf clearly, with a single layer of thick-walled epidermis covered by a thick cuticle and punctuated by depression stomata on the surface. Each stoma has two guard cells and two subsidiary cells. It opens into a vestibule cavity on the outside and a substomatal cavity on the interior. Under the epidermis is a few layers of thick sclerenchymatous hypodermis. Palisade and spongy parenchyma cannot be distinguished. The cells in this region are thin-walled, parenchymatous, polygonal, and densely packed, with chloroplasts and starch grains. The inner surface has peg-like infoldings. It is surrounded by single-layered endodermis with barrel-shaped cells and casparian strips. A T-shaped mass of sclerenchymatous cells jammed between two vascular bundles can be easily seen in a multi-layered pericycle immediately under the endodermis [55].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity studies of different living organisms
ce:section.ce:para: Additionally, the cytotoxicity and cell viability of pine leaf, mushrooms, fungus, and bacteria have been examined, as shown in Fig. 12 (a)-(d). Cell viability was more than 69% for Agaricus bisporus, 63% for Staphylococcus aureus, 75% for Mucor circinelloides, and 77% for Pinus roxburghii even at higher concentrations (200 μg/mL) for up to 4 days. This implies that CQDs are biocompatible and have minimal cytotoxicity [56].

However, it is worth mentioning that the high emission quantum yield of carbon quantum dots remains a challenge and is necessary for their use in bioimaging applications. Furthermore, the defects in carbon quantum dots play an important role in the fluorescence nature, and bioimaging sensitivity or selectivity must be improved.

In summary, fluorescent carbon quantum dots unintentionally doped with N, K, and Ca were synthesized using a hydrothermal assisted synthesis process from the extract of the nutritious fruit known as bael (Aegle marmelos) as a green source in a one-step, convenient, eco-friendly, and cost-effective manner. The as-synthesized carbon dots demonstrated strong fluorescence intensity, outstanding photostability, and effective yellow-greenish fluorescent emission with up to 4.06% quantum yield after being subjected to UV radiation. According to HRTEM and XRD investigations, the synthesized CQDs have a nearly spherical shape with an average size of 4.39 nm and are poor crystalline in nature, whereas EDX reveals unintentional doping of CQDs with Ca, K, and N without adding their corresponding precursors because the mineral present in the juice is beneficial for doping CQDs. Meanwhile, NMR spectra revealed the existence of significant sp2 and sp3 carbon groups. Furthermore, they demonstrated outstanding water solubility and a negative surface charge with a −5.29 mV zeta potential value. The impact of hydrothermal process parameters was also studied. Based on consistency and repeatability, as well as outstanding UV–Vis and fluorescence properties, the hydrothermal process parameters were optimized at 110 °C for 3 h. CQDs were also discovered to have pH sensitive photoluminescence properties. In fact, a slight blue shift was seen in pH-responsive PL spectra, which was most likely generated by reversible protonation and deprotonation of functional groups like carboxylic and amide groups. Furthermore, as-synthesized N,K,Ca-doped CQDs displayed exceptional fluorescent staining capabilities as a substitute for organic or synthetic dyes and might be employed as a valuable nanoprobe for in vitro cellular imaging of different tumors or live organisms. In contrast, SH-SY5Y (human neuroblastoma cells), HCT-116 (human colon cancer cells), and other living species such as pine leaf, mushrooms, algae, fungus, and bacteria have been studied for bioimaging, cell cytotoxicity, and cell survival. Cell survival and cytotoxicity have also been proved to be reasonable, with SH-SY5Y and HCT-116 surviving over 90%, while other live species may survive greater dosages (200 μg/mL) for up to 4 days with cell survivability ranging from 69 to 77%.
Not applicable.

Vishal Kansay: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Varun Dutt Sharma: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. G. Chandan: Investigation, Formal analysis. Indu Sharma: Investigation. Anita Bhatia: Visualization. S. Chakrabarti: Methodology, Supervision. M.K. Bera: Conceptualization, Methodology, Resources, Writing – review & editing, overall Supervision.
